coredata.dc:title: Graphdiyne/graphene quantum dots for development of FRET ratiometric fluorescent assay toward sensitive detection of miRNA in human serum and bioimaging of living cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Bahari, Delnia
Babamiri, Bahareh
Salimi, Abdollah
Rashidi, Asrin
coredata.dc:description: An effective ratiometric fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) assay developed for the sensitive and specific measurement of microRNAs-21 (miRNA-21) using graphdiyne quantum dots (GDQDs) as the new fluorescent probe. GDQDs with uniform size, narrow defined tunable emission peak and good crystallization synthesized via a classical solvothermal approach. In the sensing strategy, GDQDs modified with DNA probe exhibited strong fluorescence (FL) signal at 405 nm, while in the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) duo to Förster-resonance-energy transfer process between GDQDs and GQDs, the fluorescence of GDQDs was eﬃciently quenched, meanwhile, a new fluorescence signal at 505 nm for GQDs provided. In the presence of miRNA-21, with the hybridization between miRNA-21 and GDQDs modified with DNA probe, the distance of GDQDs (donor) and GQD (acceptor) is increased, and the intensity fluorescent signal of GDQD recovered, which confirmed by decreasing of the average life times for GDQDs-DNA probe from 1.99 to 0.61ns after addition of GQDs, while the average life time increased again to 1.26 ns in the presence of micRNA-21 target. The ratio of the FL intensity (F405/F505) behaved precise and sensitive analytical efficiency for miRNA-21 detection with a good linear range from 5 pM to 200 nM and a detection limit of 0.5 pM (S/N = 3). More importantly, this RNA sensor was feasible to detect target microRNA-21 in the human serum sample and MCF-7 cell lines with a detection limit of 4 cells/10 μL. Furthermore, due to low cytotoxicity of GDQDs multicolour imaging of MDA-MB231 living cancer cells also utilized.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence assay
MicroRNAs-21
Graphdiyne quantum dots (GDQDs)
MCF-7 cell lines

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Imaging of microRNA-21 in fixed and living MDA-MB231 cell lines
Characterization and properties study of GDY, GDQDs and GDQD-DNA probe
Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the sensor for miRNA-21 detection
Analytical application of the proposed biosensor in human serum
Cellular toxicity and living cells bioimaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the research office of the University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1404086 ) for financial support. The Financial support of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) (grant number 96002684 ) also acknowledged. We would like to thanks professor Mohamad Reza Ganjali (University of Tehran) and their collogues Professor Morteza Hossaini and Dr Foad Salehnia for life time fluorescence measurements.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the research office of the University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1404086) for financial support. The Financial support of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) (grant number 96002684) also acknowledged. We would like to thanks professor Mohamad Reza Ganjali (University of Tehran) and their collogues Professor Morteza Hossaini and Dr Foad Salehnia for life time fluorescence measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (A) GDY, (B) GDQD, (C) UV–vis absorption of GDY and GDQD, (D) FL emission spectra of GDQD with different exciting wavelengths, (E) FT-IR spectra of (a) GDY and (b) GDQD, (F) UV–vis absorption of GDQD-DNA probe, DNA probe and GDQD.

(A) TEM image of GQD, (B) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum of GQD with various excitation wavelengths, (C) FT-IR spectrum of the GQD. Effect of (D) the concentration of capture probe GDQD-DNA probe, (E) GQDs concentration, (F) hybridization reaction time between capture probe GDQD-DNA probe and target miR-21 on the FL intensity.

(A) The separated FL spectra of pure GDQD (405 nm) (a), GQD (505 nm) (b), FL response of GDQD in the presence of GQD(c), FL response of GDQD with adding the concentration of target miR-21 (d), FL intensity of the GDQD and the UV–vis spectrum of GQD (inset), The fluorescence lifetime decay spectra of GDQD (B), GDQD + GQD (C) and GDQD + GQD + target miR-21 (D) at excitation wavelength of 310 nm.

(A) FL response of GDQDs-DNA probe in the presence of various concentration of GQD from 0.01 to 0.5 mg mL−1, (B) FL response of the proposed biosensor under different miRNA-21 concentration, (C) linear relationship between FGDQD/F GQD values and the logarithm of miRNA-21 concentration from 5.0 pM to 200 nM, (D) The specificity of the proposed FL biosensor: miRNA-21 (15 nM), miRNA-155 (0.15 μM), BSA (0.15 μM), CA15-3 (0.15 μM), single base-mismatched of microRNA-21 (0.15 μM) and mixture (containing 15 nM miRNA-21 + 0.15 μM miRNA-155 + 0.15 μM BSA+0.15 μM CA15-3+0.15 single base-mismatched of microRNA-21).

Cellular cytotoxicity assessment of the GDQD (A) and GQD (B) toward HeLa cells, respectively, (C) fluorescence microscopy images of MDA-MB 231 cells incubated with GDQD-microRNA-21 under the excitation of 405 nm for all images, the scale bar is 25 μm. (D) FL response of biosensor in the presence of MCF-7 cancer cells at range 1000–50,000 cell/mL (E) The linear relationship between the FGDQD/F GQD and the cell number from 1000 to 50,000.

Schematic diagram for the fabrication of ratiometric fluorescent biosensor. (A) Preparation process of GQD, (B) Synthesis of the GDQD, (C) Schematic principle of the ratiometric fluorescent miRNA biosensor.
A. Comparison the analytical performance of different methods for the determination of microRNA-21, B. Recovery results of microRNA-21 assay for serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Graphdiyne/graphene quantum dots for development of FRET ratiometric fluorescent assay toward sensitive detection of miRNA in human serum and bioimaging of living cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, Sanandaj, 66177-15175, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, Sanandaj, 66177-15175, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Kurdistan University of Medical Sciences, Sanandaj, 66177-13446, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Kurdistan University of Medical Sciences, 66177-13446, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An effective ratiometric fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) assay developed for the sensitive and specific measurement of microRNAs-21 (miRNA-21) using graphdiyne quantum dots (GDQDs) as the new fluorescent probe. GDQDs with uniform size, narrow defined tunable emission peak and good crystallization synthesized via a classical solvothermal approach. In the sensing strategy, GDQDs modified with DNA probe exhibited strong fluorescence (FL) signal at 405 nm, while in the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) duo to Förster-resonance-energy transfer process between GDQDs and GQDs, the fluorescence of GDQDs was eﬃciently quenched, meanwhile, a new fluorescence signal at 505 nm for GQDs provided. In the presence of miRNA-21, with the hybridization between miRNA-21 and GDQDs modified with DNA probe, the distance of GDQDs (donor) and GQD (acceptor) is increased, and the intensity fluorescent signal of GDQD recovered, which confirmed by decreasing of the average life times for GDQDs-DNA probe from 1.99 to 0.61ns after addition of GQDs, while the average life time increased again to 1.26 ns in the presence of micRNA-21 target. The ratio of the FL intensity (F405/F505) behaved precise and sensitive analytical efficiency for miRNA-21 detection with a good linear range from 5 pM to 200 nM and a detection limit of 0.5 pM (S/N = 3). More importantly, this RNA sensor was feasible to detect target microRNA-21 in the human serum sample and MCF-7 cell lines with a detection limit of 4 cells/10 μL. Furthermore, due to low cytotoxicity of GDQDs multicolour imaging of MDA-MB231 living cancer cells also utilized.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs are a kind of small, endogenous and nonprotein encoding single-stranded RNAs molecule with a length of 18–25 nucleotides [1,2], which have the fundamental effects in numerous human biological processes including biological development, proliferation, genetic expression, cellular differentiation, clinical analysis and disease diagnosis [3–6]. Also, duo to high stability in serum and tissue sample, miRNAs have been considered as biomarker candidates in clinical analysis and disease diagnosis [7,8]. MicroRNA-21 (miR-21) identified as a robust oncogenic microRNA, that can be utilized as a biomarker for diagnosis of cancers such as lung adenocarcinomas, pancreatic cancer, breast cancer, glioblastoma and so on [9–11]. Due to the small size and the high similarity of sequences, it is difficult to detect the quantification of microRNA. Therefore, the sensitive and specific detection of miRNAs with economical utilize of time and cost are important, especially for early clinical diagnosis of diseases [12]. Various analytical methods for the detection of microRNAs including surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [13], electrochemistry methods [14], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [15], fluorescence [16] and surface Plasmon resonance [17] have been developed. Among these methods, the fluorescent technique has attracted more attention due to good sensitivity, technical simplicity, rapid analysis, accuracy and little damage to sample. It is well known that intensity based on the single fluorescence probe for detection may be perturbed by some factors such as environmental conditions, the concentration change of sensors and instrumental efficiency [18–20]. Nevertheless, ratiometric fluorescence sensors because of using the ratio of signal of two emission intensities under one excitation wavelength, instead of the absolute value of the single signal can reduce above limitations [21]. At present, the resonance energy transfer (RET) strategy based ratiometric fluorescence sensing has received much attention in sensing and bio-imaging. This technique was dependent on the spectral overlap between the fluorescence emission spectra of donor specie and absorption spectra of acceptor specie. Increasing the donor-acceptor pair overlap cause to enhance the energy transfer-based ratiometric fluorescence efficiency and improve the sensitivity and accuracy of the assay [22,23].

Compare to organic fluorescent probes, quantum dots (QDs) have many remarkable properties, such as better photostability against photobleaching, narrower spectral line-width and simple synthesis route [24]. Among the quantum dots reported so far, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphdiyne quantum dots (GDQD) due to significant advantages including stable emission, excellent biocompatibility, high photoluminescence and low cytotoxicity could be employed as powerful tools to investigate living cell imaging and fluorescent sensing [ [22,25–27]].

Graphdiyne (GDY), as a new kind of 2D carbon allotropes with both sp2-and sp-hybridized carbon atoms possess some of unique properties such as, the good chemical stability and highly conjugated electronic structure and more design flexibility, which could be an ideal candidate for photocatalysis, energy storage, biosensing and bioimaging design [28–31]. To date, GDY successfully synthesized for some applications in various morphologies, including GDY films, powders, nanosheets, nanowalls, nanotubes, nanowires, three-dimensional (3D) frameworks and etc. [28]. GDQDs with uniform size and good crystallization because of active acetenyl units and large surface area are used for biological application and applied in efficient and stable perovskite solar cells [31]. As a novel kind of fluorescent probe, GDQDs demonstrate significant advantages such as good photostability, resistance to photobleaching, good biocompatibility and high optical properties, which are safe for prospective application in biological systems and cellular imaging because they haven't cytotoxic effects in vitro [29,31,32]. Furthermore, GQDs as zero dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterial exhibit excellent photoluminescence, longer fluorescence lifetime and high hydrophilicity, which these properties cause to distinct GQDs from conventional graphene sheets [33–35]. By taking advantages of their fundamental properties, GQDs have explored for biological applications.

In the present study, in our best knowledge for the first time we developed a sensitive ratiometric FRET based fluorescence assay for micRNA-21 detection using GDQDs as donor and GQDs as acceptor. As shown in Scheme 1 , for the first time GDQDs modified with DNA probe exhibited the maximum fluorescence emission at wavelength of 405 nm. Due to the overlap between emission spectra of the GDQD and the absorption spectra of GQD, with introducing of GQD, the ﬂuorescence of GDQDs modified with DNA probe was quenched. Meanwhile, GQDs show a new emission peak at 505 nm. Since, the efficiency of energy transfer-between GDQD and GQD depends on their distance, in the presence of miRNA-21 target and hybridization with DNA probe labelled GDQD, the distance of GDQDs (donor) and GQD (acceptor) increased, the intensity fluorescent signal of GDQD is recovered, and intensity fluorescence of GQD decreased. The experimental results demonstrated that the ratio of the FL intensity (GDQD/GQD) changed with changing of micRNA target concentration, and this probe showed not only a good selectivity, good renewability and high sensitivity for miRNA-21 detection in the human serum sample and MCF-7 cell lines, but also it has low toxicity for bioimaging of living cells lines.

Citric acid, urea, 1,3,5-triethynylbenzene, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N- hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were ordered from Sigma-Aldrich. Dialysis tube with a molecular weight cut off:1000 Da were ordered from Sigma-Aldrich. Human serum samples for real sample analysis obtained from Noor Hematological Clinic Center, Sanandaj, Iran. The human breast cancer cell line includes MCF-7 was attained from the national cell bank of Iran (Pasteur Institute of Iran, Tehran). The detection phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.40) was prepared by using 0.10 M NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4 + 0.10 M NaCl. The phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.40) used for fluorescence studies throughout the study.
The oligonucleotide sequences provided by BIONEER, Global Genomics Partner and purified using HPLC. Their base sequence summarized below:
DNA-probe - 5’-(NH2)6 TCAACATCAGTCTGATAAGCT-3’.
microRNA -target-5′-UAG CUU AUC AGA GUG AUG UUGA-3'.
microRNA -mismatched-5′-UAG CUU AUC ACA CUG AUG UUG A-3'.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum performed on a Varian Cary 5000 UV–vis–NIR absorption spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) by using the program Win ASPECT version 2.3.10. The Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) applied for fluorescence (FL) measurements. Fluorescence images of cells obtained by using an Olympus microscope (FV1000, Japan). MTT assay carried out utilizing a microplate reader (Synergy 2, Biotek, USA). The absorbance for 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was obtained using a Tecan's Infinite M200 microplate reader at a wavelength of 570 nm. Fourier Transform-Infrared (FT-IR) spectra performed from a Vector-22 BRUKER spectrophotometer (Switzerland). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were attained using a H600 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan).

Firstly, the graphdiyne was prepared based on our recently approached [36], using 1,3,5-triethynylbenzene as precursor [37], then GDQDs was prepared by hydrothermal treatment. Firstly, graphdiyne (1 mg) was oxidized in concentrated HNO3 (6 mL) and H2SO4 (2 mL) for 20 h under ultrasonication condition (500 W, 40 kHz). Then, the obtained mixture diluted to 50 mL with deionized (DI) water and filtered with a 0.22-μm microporous membrane to eliminate the acids. In the following, 0.04 g purified oxidized graphdiyne were re-dispersed in 8 mL of DI water, and the pH was reached to 8 by using NaOH. Subsequently, the obtained suspension moved to a Teflon autoclave for the hydrothermal reaction. After heating treatment for 10 h at 200 °C and cooling down to room temperature, the attained product filtered with a 0.22-μm microporous membrane. Finally, the light yellow solution was dialyzed using dialysis tubing membrane overnight, and the obtained graphdiyne quantum dots were stored at 4 °C.

GQDs were prepared similar to the prior method with some modifications. Briefly, 0.17 g of urea and 0.105 g of citric acid dissolved in 5 mL of dimethylformamide (DMF) and keep stirring until a clear solution obtained. Subsequently, the mixture moved into poly-(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the attained brown solution by adding hexane was centrifuged at (5000 rpm, 10 min) for three times, the purified GQDs finally attained.

The 5׳-amine modiﬁed of the DNA probe covalently attached to the carboxyl group of the GDQDs. Firstly, 1 mL of the GDQDs (1 mg mL−1) were first activated in 1 mL PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.40), which contains 20 mg EDC and 10 mg NHS. Then, 100 μL of 500 nM DNA probe added to the above solution stored for 24 h at the refrigerator. Finally, GDQDs-DNA probe was stored at 4 °C for further utilize.

The fluorescence assays were carried out in about 300 μL of reaction solution containing GDQDs-DNA probe (200 μL), GQDs (100 μL, 0.4 mg mL−1), and 10 μL PBS solution (0.1 M, pH 7.40) containing various concentrations of target microRNA- 21 (5 pM–200 nM). Ultimately, ratiometrically fluorescence intensity for various concentrations of microRNA-21 after incubation for 20 min recorded. The recorded FL emission utilized for detection of target microRNA.

MCF-7 cells (human breast adreno carcinoma) were cultured in RPMI 1640 growth medium supplemented with 10 % FBS (Fetal Bovine Serum) and 100 IU. mL−1 penicillin-streptomycin. Also, MDA-MB231 cells were seeded in DMEM supplemented with 10 % (v/v) FBS and 100 IU. mL−1 penicillin-streptomycin. The cells were held in CO2 incubator at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere (95 % air and 5 % CO2) in tissue culture flask.

For assessing the cell viability, MTT Cell Proliferation and Cytotoxicity Assay Kit were used. The breast cancer cells (HeLa) (5 × 103 cells. mL−1) were seed in 96-well plates in the culture medium for one day under a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. Subsequently, 2–20 μL of GQDs (0.04 g mL−1)-GDQDs (0.1 g mL−1) was added to each well containing 990 μL of culture medium. Completion of incubation period, 10 μL of 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (5.0 mg. mL−1) were added into the 96-well plate (10 mL per well) and incubated for at 37 °C for 2 h. In the following, 100 μL of Formazan solution added to each well and incubated for 4 h for dissolving crystals formed in living cells. Finally, the absorbance of each well calculate for measuring the cell viability on a microplate reader at 570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of microRNA-21 in fixed and living MDA-MB231 cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: The usage of GDQDs probe for microRNA-21 measurement in cells appraised in MDA-MB231 cells. For the assays, the cells (1 x 103 cells per well in 100 μL of Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) containing 10 % serum), were seeded in a 96-well culture dishes. After overnight plating, culture media treated with a fresh solution of GDQDs-DNA probe for 20 min, and then the cell cultures washed with phosphate buffer to remove the free nanoprobe. Finally, images were taken by fluorescence microscopy (Olympus IX51, Japan) with 405 nm excitation, and the fluorescence from 420 to 510 nm, or from 560 nm to 520 nm–560 nm and 590 nm–750 nm for GDQDs were collected, respectively.

The fluorescence detection process is indicated in Scheme 1. Firstly, GDQDs as fluorophore was covalently attached to DNA probe, which showed the strong fluorescence emission at 405 nm. When GQDs was added to the GDQDs-DNA probe, GDQDs in contact with DNA probe was near to the surface of the GQDs and GDQDs-DNA probe/GQDs assemblies were formed, then the fluorescence of GDQDs due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between GDQDs as donor species and GQDs as acceptors was quenched. In the presence of microRNA target, with the hybridization reaction between GDQDs-DNA probe and target microRNA-21, the distance between the GDQDs-DNA probe and GQDs is increased, which gives rise to decrease the efficiency of FRET from the GDQDs to the GQDs and the recovery of the fluorescence signal of GDQDs and decrease of the fluorescence signal of GQDs. Subsequently, the changes in fluorescence ratio intensity are relevant to the concentration of microRNA-21. So, this strategy can be developed to measurement of microRNA-21.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and properties study of GDY, GDQDs and GDQD-DNA probe
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image was employed for characterization of the structures and morphological features of as-prepared GDY and GDQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the graphdiyne was prepared of uniform and continuous microstructure with accumulated layers. The GDQDs synthesized by breakage of graphdiyne (GDY) nanosheets. The morphological features of the GDQDs observed in Fig. 1B. As seen, the GDQDs with the hexagonal carbon network has the similar-spherical structure with a mean size of 4–6 nm. The UV–vis spectrum and photoluminescence (PL) spectra recorded to characterize the optical properties of prepared GDY and GDQDs. As indicated in Fig. 1C, the GDY aqueous solution has an observable absorption band concentrated at 305 nm. In addition, the absorption band of GDQDs observed at this wavelength by higher absorption band intensity, which can be assigned to n−π* transition of CO bonds. Furthermore, the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission of GDQDs recorded at diverse excitation wavelengths from 200 nm to 370 nm (Fig. 1D). It can see that the maximum excitation of the GDQDs was at 330 nm, and at this excitation wavelength, the maximum fluorescence signal obtained at 405 nm, then the PL emission intensity gradually decreased. The infrared peaks of the GDQDs illustrated in Fig. 1E, the band located at ~3450 cm−1 indicates the being of O–H stretching vibrations. Also, the bands of 2959 and 2927 cm−1 are the typical C–H stretching vibration. Moreover, absorptions at 1664.73 and 1459 cm−1 can be attributed to CO and C–N bonds, respectively, that showed the existence of amide in GDQDs. The peak observed at 2126 cm−1 is ascribed to the CC stretching vibration, and also this peak appeared at 2039 in GDY as a regent. In comparison with GDY, the intensity of CC stretching vibration in GDQDs because of the molecular perfectly symmetry is a little weak. GDQDs are highly water soluble that this result demonstrated the existence of carboxyl groups on their surface. The successful immobilization of DNA probe on GDQD was also conﬁrmed using the optical absorption spectra of GDQD, DNA probe and GDQDs-DNA probe (Fig. 1F). The UV–vis absorption peak of GDQD and DNA probe observed at about 305 nm and 254 nm, respectively. With covalence interaction between GDQD and DNA probe, the same peaks at 305 nm and 254 nm observed that confirmed the successful loading of DNA probe on GDQD. Also, to further investigate the loading of DNA probe on GDQD, the FT-IR spectrum and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded. As shown in Fig S1, the peak at 1554 cm−1 in the spectrum of GDQD-DNA probe corresponded to –CON, indicated the coordination of the amino groups of DNA probe with the carboxyl group of GDQD. Furthermore, the successful immobilization of DNA probe on GDQD was demonstrated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and the results are shown in Fig.S2. As illustrated, the bare ITO electrode revealed a small semicircle (curve a). After the immobilization of nafion/GDQD on the surface of the electrode, the resistance increased (curve b). In the following, increase of the resistance value was obtained by the incubation of nafion/GDQD-DNA probe (curve c). The obtained EIS results confirmed the successful immobilization of DNA probe on GDQD.

TEM performed to acquire the morphology of GQD. As shown in Fig. 2 A, spherical GQD homogeneously dispersed and had a small size with an average diameter of 5–7 nm. The optical properties of GQDs illustrated in Fig. 2B. The GQD illustrated a significant UV–vis absorption band by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy at approximately 420 nm. The PL spectrum of GQD investigated at variant excitation wavelengths from 210 nm to 550 nm that the maximum fluorescence emission intensity, upon exciting at 440 nm, centered at approximately 505 nm. To characterize the functional groups and chemical structure of the GQD, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum recorded. As seen in Fig. 2C, the absorption band at 3409 and 3206 cm−1 corresponds to stretching of the primary amino group (NH2). The absorption peaks at 2939,1713, 1661 and 1275 cm−1 are indicative of C–H stretching, NH2 scissoring, CC and C–N bonds, respectively. The appearance of these peaks compared to citric acid as a reagent indicates the formation of GQD.

The experimental conditions for the ratiometric fluorescence sensor optimized to obtain the best sensing performances for miRNA-21 detection. The assay conditions such as pH of experimental, the appropriate concentration of GQDs and GDQDs-DNA probe, the hybridization reaction time between capture GDQDs-DNA probe and target miRNA-21. As illustrated in Fig.S3 the best response was obtained at pH 7.40. So, pH 7.40 was selected as the optimal pH value in proposed bioassay. As indicated in Fig. 2D, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity (FGDQD/FGQDs) increased gradually and tended to a constant value with the increase of GDQDs-DNA probe concentration. Therefore, 200 μL selected as the optimal GDQDs-DNA probe concentration for the detection of miRNA-21. Next, as illustrated in Fig. 2E, the ratio values decreased with increasing the concentration of GQDs, and then tended to a constant value after 0.4 mg mL−1. Thus, 0.4 mg mL−1 of GQDs utilized as a certain concentration of GQDs in this study. The hybridization reaction time between GDQDs-DNA probe and target miRNA-21 optimized by recording of fluorescence emission at the range 5–35 min. With increasing incubation time, the FL intensity gradually increased and finally at 20 min reached the maximum value and then tended to reach equilibrium (Fig. 2F).

To indicate the facility of the ratiometric strategy in miRNA bioassay, FL intensity of GDQD, GQD, as well as the mixture of quantum dots were measured (Fig. 3 A). Firstly, the distinctly separated FL signals for pure GDQD (405 nm) and GQD (505 nm) could supply the separation of the dual signals (spectra “a" and “b"). Then, DNA sequences covalently attached to GDQDs via reaction between –COOH group of GDQDs and –NH2 group of DNA probe. When GQDs were added into the system, the fluorescence of GQD increased gradually while that fluorescence of GDQDs-DNA probe due to the Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) decreased (spectrum c). Because, the absorption spectrum of the GQD (accepter specie) had an excellent spectral overlap with the fluorescence emission of the GDQDs (donor species), the FL response of GDQDs eﬀectively quenched (inset of Fig. 3A). The specific hybridizing between GDQDs-DNA probe and target miRNA-21 happened that the formed miRNA-DNA probe complex with the rigid double helix structures can increase the distance between GDQDs-DNA probe and GQD within 20 nm. With increasing the concentration of target miRNA-21 and increasing the distance between donor and acceptor, the efficiency of FRET process decreased, which caused the restoration of the fluorescence signal of GDQD (spectrum “d"). To further investigate the mechanism of interaction between GDQD and GQD, time-resolved experiment carried out to measure the fluorescence lifetime of GDQD in the presence of GQD and target miRNA-21 (Fig. 3B and C). For these experiments, fluorescence was collected at the emission wavelength of 405 nm with excitation wavelength of 310 nm, which correspond to the emission maxima of GDQD. As can be seen, the average fluorescence lifetime of probe GDQD was 1.99 ns and decreased to 0.61 ns in presence of GQDs. On the contrary, after occurring of the hybridization reaction between GDQDs-DNA probe and target miRNA-21 the average fluorescence lifetime of GDQDs increased again from 0.61 ns to 1.26 ns (Fig. 3D). Decreasing the lifetime for GDQDs indicate that the FRET process occurred in the presence of GQD, while after hybridization of micRNA-target with GDQDs-DNA probe the distance between GQDs and GDQDs increased, which decreased the energy transfer efficiency in FRET process.

The response of the ratiometric fluorescent towards target miRNA-21 examined in detail under optimized conditions. Initially, by increasing concentrations of GQD to the system (0.01–0.5 mg mL−1), the intensity of GDQDs-DNA probe at 405 nm according to the theory of FRET decreased continuously, while the FL intensity of GQDs at 505 nm significantly increased (Fig. 4 A). Upon addition of various concentrations of miRNA-21 probe, restoration of the fluorescence signal of GDQDs-DNA probe at 405 nm duo to increase the distance between GDQDs-DNA probe and GQD obtained. As shown in Fig. 4B and C, the FL intensity ratio of GDQD and GQD was proportionate to the logarithm of varied miRNA-21 concentrations in the range of 5 pM–200 nM. A linear regression equation of FGDQD/FGQD = 0.8499 + 0.2616 log CmiRNA-21 (R2 = 0.9889) was obtained with a detection limit of 0.5 pM (S/N = 3). The working performance of this suggested method, such as linear concentration range and detection limit found to be comparable or better than the other measurement protocols (Table 1 A). So, the obtained results indicated that the ratiometric fluorescence method showed a higher sensitivity and reliability method for miRNA-21 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the sensor for miRNA-21 detection
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is an important factor in evaluating the performance of ratiometric fluorescence biosensing assay during target analysis for practical applications. To explore the selectivity of above ratiometric biosensor, different biologically relevant chemical substances such as CA15-3, BSA, microRNA-155, single base-mismatched of microRNA-21 and mixture sample that contains miRNA-21, miRNA-155, BSA, CA15-3 and single base-mismatched of microRNA-21 were applied. As presented in Fig. 4D, only in the presence of the target miRNA-21, the fluorescence intensity ratio Δ[FGDQD/FGQD] significantly changed, suggesting the stronger interactions between target miRNA-21 and GDQDs-DNA probe. These data indicated the high selectivity of biosensor towards miRNA-21 analysis. To further explore the FL stabilities, after keeping GDQDs-DNA probe/GQD in the presence of fixed concentration of miRNA-at 4 °C during 3 weeks, the fluorescence intensity ratio Δ[FGDQD/FGQD] of the measuring system remained almost 90 % of the initial response. Also, the GDQDs/GQDs exhibited slight photobleaching during continuous excitation at 310 nm for 5 h (Fig.S4), indicating outstanding photostability, which is necessary for biosensing applications. Furthermore, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of current intensity of FGDQD/FGQD value for ﬁve independent assays was 2.05 %, implying that the as-proposed biosensor exhibited the suitable reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application of the proposed biosensor in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application of this assay, the standard addition method used for the analysis of miR-21 in the real sample and the obtained results compared to reported values by ELISA kits as a standard method in the clinical laboratory. The human serum samples obtained from the Noor Clinical laboratory in Sanandaj of Iran. In this study, we used two human serum samples contains different values of microRNAs (0.1 and 15 nM) as a real sample. To access the practicability of the biosensor, the human serum samples were spiked with different concentrations of microRNA-21 (1, 5, 10 and 50 nM). As shown in Table 1B, the favorable recoveries ranged 97.9–103.3% (n = 3) were obtained, which indicate the suggested ratiometric fluorescent method has high accuracy and acceptable detection performance for microRNA-21 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular toxicity and living cells bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: For further evaluation of the prepared QDs in response to varying cellular components, the toxicity of QDs should be investigated. The characteristic cell cytotoxicity of GQDs and GDQDs toward HeLa cells evaluated, using MTT assay method. The experimental results in Fig. 5 A and B showed that cell viability has not significantly changed for 24 h under different QDs concentrations. Inherent toxicity of GDQDs for low doses (0.2 mg mL−1), medium, (1 mg mL−1), and high doses (2 mg mL−1) toward HeLa cells and inherent toxicity of GQDs for low doses (0.08 mg mL−1), medium, (0.45 mg mL−1), and high doses (0.8 mg mL−1) toward HeLa cells were measured (Fig. 5A). The results demonstrate that more than 90 % of HeLa cells at low concentrations are viable, undoubtedly showing low cytotoxicity. The slight cytotoxicity and high fluorescence stability of these QDs cause to suitable of these luminophores for bioimaging applications. When GDQDs-DNA probe (100 μL) for 15 min incubated to live MDA-MB 231 cells lines, under the excitation of 405 nm, the MDA-MB 231 cells emitted strong blue (420–510 nm), green (560-520 nm), yellow (590-560 nm), and red fluorescence (590–750 nm) (Fig. 5C). This result showed that GDQD-binding microRNA-121 is stable, and demonstrates its effective potential in bioimaging of targeted cancer cells.

To evaluate the performance of suggested ratiometric fluorescence biosensing assay in biomedical fields, microRNA-21 in MCF-7 cells was measured with the proposed bioassay. As shown in Fig. 5D, when MCF-7 cells concentration range increased from 1000 to 50,000 cell mL−1, fluorescence response ratio of (FGDQD/FGQD) increased, and the linear regression equation was y (FL) = 0.0176x (cell number) +0.5389 with regression of R2 = 0.9849 (Fig. 5E). The calculated detection limit was evaluated to be 400 cell mL−1 (4 cells in 10 μL of the injected sample) at a S/N of 3.

In summary, we have designed a simple ratiometric fluorescent method for specific and sensitive detection of target miRNA-21 based on GQDs as a fluorescence “off-on” switch for new probe GDQDs through resonance energy transfer. A water-soluble GDQDs with good photostability, derived from graphdiyne, evaluated as a fluorescence probe for the detection of miRNA-21 in biological samples. Herein, GQDs acted as energy acceptor and quenched the fluorescence of DNA probe labelled GDQDs and fluorescence ratio response of GDQDs-DNA/GQDs measured as response for proposed assay. Hybridization of the target micRNA with GDQDs-DNA probe lead to the intensity recovery of fluorescence attenuation of GDQD, and the fluorescence ratio intensity of (FGDQD/FGQD) measured as a signal response. The proposed assay used for miRNA-21 detection at concentration range 5 pM to 200 nM with detection limit of 0.5pM. Meanwhile, due to the simplicity, selectivity, good repeatability, and high sensitivity of the assay, it has applied for the detection of miRNA-21 in serum samples and also analysis of MCF-7 cell lines. Furthermore, the low cytotoxicity of GDQDs provided multicolour imaging of MDA-MB231 living cancer cells. The considerable performance of the GDQDs as fluorescence probe with low cytotoxicity promised for development of sensitive, robust biosensors for determination of other microRNA sequences to meet clinical needs in the diagnosis of covid-19.

Delnia Bahari; doing the experiment, writing and data analysis, Bahareh Babamiri; synthesis and characterization of materials, real sample analysis and bioimaging; Abdollah Salimi Supervision, reviewing and Editing, Asrin Rashidi, micoRNA measuring in breast cancer cells.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the research office of the University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1404086) for financial support. The Financial support of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) (grant number 96002684) also acknowledged. We would like to thanks professor Mohamad Reza Ganjali (University of Tehran) and their collogues Professor Morteza Hossaini and Dr Foad Salehnia for life time fluorescence measurements.

The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2021.118371.
